q: Why does this loop not save items in order?

opts.t: forEach ignores the returned promises
save() must be wrapped in try/catch
opts.why: Missing try/catch would cause unhandled rejections, but that's a separate issue—forEach never awaits the callback regardless.

opts.t: Nothing — forEach awaits each async callback
opts.why: forEach is not promise-aware; it fires each callback and moves on without awaiting.

why: forEach does not await the promise returned by an async callback, so 'done' logs before any save finishes and errors go silently unhandled. Use for...of with await, or Promise.all(arr.map(save)).

q: What happens when fetchData() rejects here?

opts.t: Rejection is caught because await p is present
opts.why: The await only wraps the resolution—if fetchData rejects before doOtherWork finishes, the rejection is unhandled until the await, but a missing try/catch still crashes in Node ≥ 15.

opts.t: Node.js crashes — no try/catch wraps await p
fetchData() runs synchronously before doOtherWork
opts.why: Calling an async function starts it and returns a promise immediately; execution yields at the first internal await.

opts.t: doOtherWork() is skipped because of the floating promise
opts.why: doOtherWork() runs synchronously right after fetchData() returns its promise.

why: Since Node.js 15, an unhandled promise rejection terminates the process. Without try/catch around 'await p', any rejection thrown by fetchData() crashes the app. Wrap in try/catch or add .catch().
fj: "The await is there, so rejections are safe."
fs: "Since Node 15, unhandled rejections crash. Always try/catch your awaits."

q: Three independent DB calls run sequentially. Best fix?

opts.t: const [a,b,c] = await Promise.all([getUser(), getOrders(), getProds()])
const a = getUser(), b = getOrders(), c = getProds(); return {a,b,c}
opts.why: This returns unresolved promises, not the data.

opts.t: Run each inside setImmediate to free the event loop
opts.why: setImmediate does not parallelize I/O-bound async calls; Promise.all is the correct tool.

why: Sequential awaits serialize independent I/O—total latency is the sum of all three. Promise.all runs all three in parallel, reducing latency to the slowest single call.
fj: "Sequential awaits are fine since each awaits completion anyway."
fs: "Promise.all fires all three concurrently—latency drops to max(a,b,c) instead of a+b+c."

q: What's wrong with this error handler?

opts.t: throw inside a callback is uncaught and crashes Node
fs.readFile should be promisified first
opts.why: Promisifying is a style choice, not the root bug; the real problem is throwing in an async callback.

opts.t: cb should be called as cb(err) instead
opts.why: cb(err) is the correct Node.js callback convention—but the actual bug is the throw, which bypasses that convention entirely.

why: Throwing inside a callback (called from an async OS operation) lands in a context with no try/catch, triggering 'uncaughtException' and crashing the process. Pass the error to the callback: cb(err) and return.
fj: "throw works the same inside callbacks as in synchronous code."
fs: "Never throw in async callbacks—call cb(err) or reject a promise instead."

q: What is the console output order?

opts.t: D, A, C, B
D, B, A, C
opts.why: setImmediate fires in the check phase, after nextTick and microtasks, so B comes last.

opts.t: A, D, C, B
opts.why: Synchronous console.log('D') always runs before any deferred callbacks.

opts.t: D, C, A, B
opts.why: process.nextTick callbacks drain before promise microtasks, so A precedes C.

why: Execution order: synchronous code (D) → nextTick queue (A) → microtask/promise queue (C) → check phase / setImmediate (B). nextTick drains before promises, and both drain before setImmediate.
fj: "Promises run before nextTick because they're newer."
fs: "nextTick → microtasks (Promises) → setImmediate. Sync code always runs first."

q: Why does this HTTP server become unresponsive under load?

opts.t: JSON.parse on a large buffer blocks the event loop
res.end must be called asynchronously
opts.why: res.end can be called synchronously; the problem is the blocking parse, not the response call.

opts.t: getHugeBuf() returns a Buffer, not a string
opts.why: JSON.parse coerces its argument; type mismatch is not the root performance problem here.

why: JSON.parse and JSON.stringify are synchronous and O(n). On large payloads they block the single-threaded event loop, stalling all other requests. Offload to a worker_thread or stream-parse instead.
fj: "JSON.parse is fast enough for any payload in practice."
fs: "Large synchronous JSON ops block the event loop. Offload to worker_threads or use a streaming JSON parser."

q: The writable stream buffers memory unboundedly. Fix?

opts.t: Check write() return; pause readable on false, resume on 'drain'
Use a larger highWaterMark on the writable
opts.why: A bigger buffer delays the problem but does not eliminate unbounded memory growth; the producer must still respect backpressure.

opts.t: Replace with readable.pipe(writable)
opts.why: pipe() handles backpressure correctly and is the idiomatic fix, but the question asks to fix the manual listener code without switching to pipe.

why: writable.write() returns false when the internal buffer exceeds highWaterMark. The producer must pause the readable at that point and resume only after the 'drain' event, otherwise data accumulates in memory without bound.
fs: "Manual 'data' listeners bypass backpressure. Pause on write()===false, resume on 'drain'—or just use pipe()."

q: This async function's error never reaches the caller. Why?

opts.t: throw inside .catch() creates a new unhandled rejection
Errors are swallowed because .catch() is present
opts.why: .catch() catches the original rejection, but rethrowing inside it creates a new rejected promise that nothing handles.

opts.t: cb should be called inside .catch(), not throw
opts.why: Calling cb(e) is the correct fix, but the question asks why the error never reaches the caller—the reason is the rethrow creates an unhandled rejection.

why: Re-throwing inside .catch() produces a new rejected promise returned by .catch() itself. No handler is attached to that promise, so Node.js emits 'unhandledRejection'. The error never reaches cb. Fix: call cb(e) in .catch().
fj: "throw inside .catch() propagates the error up the call stack to the caller."
fs: "Re-throw in .catch() creates a new unhandled rejection. Call cb(err) to forward errors in callback-style code."
